fr.title: Le premier emploi et l'argent
fr.scenario: Tu obtiens ton premier emploi à 22 ans. Première décision financière ?

choice_liability.label: S'installer confortablement : appartement sympa, voiture, sorties
choice_liability.feedback: Le piège du premier salaire : la lifestyle inflation dès le départ. Les habitudes établies à 22 ans seront difficiles à changer à 30. Commencer petit et investir la différence est une stratégie bien documentée.

choice_asset.label: Automatiser 20% du salaire en investissement dès le premier mois, avant de s'habituer au montant
choice_asset.feedback: Se payer en premier est le principe de Kiyosaki. Avant de s'habituer au salaire complet, allouer automatiquement une partie à l'investissement crée une habitude fondatrice.

fr.title: Le respect de l'argent
fr.scenario: Tu reçois $20 en trop dans ta monnaie au supermarché. Que fais-tu ?

choice_liability.label: Tu gardes — ce n'est pas ta faute si le caissier s'est trompé
choice_liability.feedback: La question de valeurs et pas seulement financière. Mais au-delà de l'éthique, comment tu traites l'argent des petites sommes reflète ta relation à l'argent en général.

choice_asset.label: Tu le signales et rends la monnaie — l'intégrité s'applique aussi aux petits montants
choice_asset.feedback: Kiyosaki parle de respecter l'argent dans toutes ses formes. L'intégrité financière dans les petites choses est le fondement de la confiance en soi sur les grandes décisions.

fr.title: La mentalité E→S→B→I
fr.scenario: Tu es salarié et veux devenir 'propriétaire d'actifs'. Par où commences-tu ?

choice_liability.label: Quitter immédiatement son emploi pour lancer quelque chose
choice_liability.feedback: Le saut immédiat du E au B sans filet est risqué. La plupart des entrepreneurs qui réussissent ont préparé leur transition en parallèle de leur emploi, pas en quittant brusquement.

choice_asset.label: Construire progressivement : E → side project S → business B → actifs passifs I en parallèle
choice_asset.feedback: Le quadrant de Kiyosaki n'est pas un saut — c'est une progression. Chaque étape construit sur la précédente. La transition se prépare pendant qu'on est encore en sécurité.

fr.title: Le refus de la médiocrité
fr.scenario: Tu te demandes si viser la liberté financière n'est pas arrogant ou irréaliste. Est-ce que ça t'arrête ?

choice_liability.label: Un peu, oui — qui suis-je pour vouloir plus que la moyenne ?
choice_liability.feedback: La normalisation de la médiocrité financière est culturellement construite. Se demander 'qui suis-je pour vouloir plus ?' est une limite mentale qui a un coût financier réel sur des décennies.

choice_asset.label: Non — viser la liberté financière n'est pas arrogant, c'est responsable
choice_asset.feedback: La liberté financière n'est pas un luxe — c'est la capacité de choisir son temps, ses projets et ses contributions. Y aspirer est un acte de responsabilité envers soi-même et ses proches.

fr.title: La définition du succès
fr.scenario: Comment définis-tu le succès financier ? Salaire élevé ou liberté de temps ?

choice_liability.label: Salaire élevé — la reconnaissance et la rémunération prouvent la valeur
choice_liability.feedback: Un salaire élevé est un succès dans le quadrant E. Mais Kiyosaki questionne : si tu arrêtes de travailler demain, ton niveau de vie change-t-il ? Si oui, tu n'es pas encore financièrement libre.

choice_asset.label: Liberté de temps — avoir des actifs qui couvrent mes dépenses sans que je travaille
choice_asset.feedback: C'est la définition de Kiyosaki : la liberté financière, c'est quand tes actifs génèrent plus que tes dépenses. Le salaire est un moyen, pas une fin. La liberté de temps est la destination.

fr.title: Le budget alimentaire inattendu
fr.scenario: Tu réalises que tu dépenses $400/mois en nourriture et livraisons, soit $200 de plus que prévu. Que fais-tu ?

choice_liability.label: C'est normal, la vie coûte cher — j'ajuste mon budget à la hausse
choice_liability.feedback: Ajuster le budget à la hausse pour justifier une dépense non optimisée est le mouvement inverse de la gestion financière. C'est le budget qui guide les dépenses, pas l'inverse.

choice_asset.label: Identifier les $200 de fuites (livraisons impulsives, gaspillage) et les rediriger
choice_asset.feedback: $200/mois = $2 400/an = un investissement significatif. Analyser les fuites et les réorienter vers un actif est une décision de quelques minutes pour un impact annuel majeur.

en.title: The Unexpected Bonus
en.scenario: You receive a $3,000 work bonus. What do you do?

choice_liability.label: Buy the latest smartphone + AirPods
choice_liability.feedback: You just turned $3,000 into something worth $1,800 tomorrow. Kiyosaki calls this feeding the liability column.

choice_asset.label: Invest in an index fund generating monthly income
choice_asset.feedback: Your bonus now works for you every month. That's the asset reflex Philia Vault trains you to build.

en.title: The Raise
en.scenario: You get a $400/month raise. How do you handle the surplus?

choice_liability.label: Upgrade your apartment and get a new car
choice_liability.feedback: Lifestyle inflation: your expenses absorbed the raise before it created anything. Net cashflow stays at zero.

choice_asset.label: Automate a $350 transfer to an investment account
choice_asset.feedback: Automation is the wealthy person's secret. You never see the money, it never gets spent — it compounds.

en.title: The Christmas Bonus
en.scenario: You receive a $1,500 year-end bonus. Party time?

choice_liability.label: Organize a big holiday dinner and gifts for everyone
choice_liability.feedback: Generous, but that money vanished in 48 hours. The experience is real, the asset is zero.

choice_asset.label: Invest $1,000 and use $500 for the holidays
choice_asset.feedback: Perfect balance. Enjoy without sacrificing your future. The 80/20 rule applied to a bonus.

en.title: The Tax Refund
en.scenario: You receive a $2,200 tax refund. First reaction?

choice_liability.label: Book an all-inclusive vacation — you deserve it
choice_liability.feedback: Precious memories, but no asset created. In 30 days the money is gone and the debts continue.

choice_asset.label: Pay off a high-interest debt or build an emergency fund
choice_asset.feedback: Paying off costly debt is a guaranteed return equal to its interest rate. Mathematically unbeatable short-term.

en.title: The Birthday Fund
en.scenario: Friends pool $800 for your birthday. What do you do?

choice_liability.label: Take everyone out to a nice restaurant to celebrate
choice_liability.feedback: Great experience — and the money evaporated. The asset column didn't move.

choice_asset.label: Buy fractional shares of productive assets
choice_asset.feedback: You transform a gift into recurring income. Your friends gave you an asset without knowing it.

en.title: The Monthly Extra
en.scenario: Your employer increases your meal allowance by $150/month. How do you use the difference?

choice_liability.label: Eat at better restaurants every day
choice_liability.feedback: You raised your lifestyle, not your net worth. Every month, $150 disappears into immediate pleasure.

choice_asset.label: Bring your lunch box and invest the $150 each month
choice_asset.feedback: $150/month for 10 years, even at a modest return, is significant. Consistency beats intensity.

en.title: The Freelance Gig
en.scenario: You land a freelance project worth $2,500 this month. First decision?

choice_liability.label: Reward yourself with purchases you've been putting off
choice_liability.feedback: You worked twice as hard to consume twice as much. The hamster runs faster but the wheel spins the same way.

choice_asset.label: Invest 80% and use 20% however you want
choice_asset.feedback: The golden rule for extra income. 80% builds your freedom, 20% rewards the effort. The virtuous cycle starts here.

en.title: The Garage Sale
en.scenario: You sell unused stuff and pocket $400. What next?

choice_liability.label: Seize the opportunity to buy new things on sale
choice_liability.feedback: You sold liabilities to buy liabilities. A perfect swap — but your situation hasn't changed.

choice_asset.label: Deposit it immediately into your investment account
choice_asset.feedback: You just converted dormant liabilities (old stuff) into productive capital. This is exactly the move Kiyosaki teaches.

en.title: The Card Cashback
en.scenario: You accumulate $250 in cashback over the year. What do you do?

choice_liability.label: Use the cashback for a fancy dinner out
choice_liability.feedback: A deserved pleasure, but the money doesn't work for you.

choice_asset.label: Reinvest the cashback into a productive asset
choice_asset.feedback: Even $250 invested regularly builds a mental habit more valuable than the dollar amount. The investor identity is forged by small acts.

en.title: The Reduced Rent
en.scenario: Your landlord drops your rent by $200/month for 6 months. What do you do with the difference?

choice_liability.label: Treat yourself to small extras — streaming, delivery, outings
choice_liability.feedback: The ease absorbs the surplus. In 6 months, you'll have nothing to show for those 6 months of 'savings'.

choice_asset.label: Automate a $200 transfer on the 1st of every month
choice_asset.feedback: You never saw this money. It went directly to build your future. The best way to spend less is to never see the money arrive.

en.title: The Insurance Negotiation
en.scenario: You renegotiate your insurance and save $80/month. Where does the surplus go?

choice_liability.label: Mentally fold it into your 'leisure' budget
choice_liability.feedback: The invisible expense. Unallocated money always ends up in unplanned spending.

choice_asset.label: Create an automatic $80 transfer to an asset this month
choice_asset.feedback: Every saving is a disguised investment opportunity. You didn't cut your budget — you reallocated it smartly.

en.title: The Friend's Repayment
en.scenario: A friend pays back $300 they owed you. Reflex?

choice_liability.label: The perfect occasion to buy something you've wanted for a while
choice_liability.feedback: Unexpected money always goes to unplanned expenses without a preset rule.

choice_asset.label: Apply your rule: all unexpected money goes straight into assets
choice_asset.feedback: Discipline on small amounts forges habits for large ones. A clear rule eliminates temptation.

en.title: The Sales Commission
en.scenario: You land an exceptional $1,800 commission this month. Action plan?

choice_liability.label: Celebrate with outings and impulse purchases
choice_liability.feedback: Celebration is natural. But if every success is immediately spent, success builds nothing.

choice_asset.label: Celebrate modestly and invest the rest for recurring income
choice_asset.feedback: The commission is an event. The passive income it generates is permanent. Turn the exceptional into structural.

en.title: The Coop Dividend
en.scenario: You receive $120 in dividends from your buying coop. What do you do?

choice_liability.label: Use the dividends for this month's groceries
choice_liability.feedback: Dividends spent on consumption never grow. The growth cycle is broken at the first withdrawal.

choice_asset.label: Automatically reinvest dividends to buy more shares
choice_asset.feedback: Compound interest in action. Each reinvested dividend generates future dividends. This is the wealth engine.

en.title: Leftover Leisure Budget
en.scenario: You realize you only spent $150 of your $300 leisure budget this month. What do you do with the $150?

choice_liability.label: Roll it over to next month to 'catch up'
choice_liability.feedback: The 'budget to spend' mentality turns savings into permission to spend more later.

choice_asset.label: Immediately transfer the unspent $150 to your investment
choice_asset.feedback: The saving made is a potential asset. Acting immediately prevents the temptation to spend it 'later'.

en.title: Lending to a Friend
en.scenario: You have $500 available. A friend asks to borrow it 'to get through the month'. What do you do?

choice_liability.label: Lend without hesitation, they're family/a friend
choice_liability.feedback: Generosity is a value. But an informal loan is often a disguised gift that creates relationship tension.

choice_asset.label: Assess your situation, be clear whether it's a loan or a gift, document it
choice_asset.feedback: Clarity and honesty protect both the relationship and your finances. An acknowledged gift is healthier than a loan with no return.

en.title: The Daily Coffee
en.scenario: You spend $5/day on coffee. A colleague says that's $150/month 'wasted'. Are they right?

choice_liability.label: They're right, I'll cut coffee immediately to save
choice_liability.feedback: Total deprivation is rarely sustainable. Financial management is optimization, not punishment.

choice_asset.label: Keep the coffee but audit my real unintentional expenses
choice_asset.feedback: The daily coffee is often the only conscious expense. The real budget leaks are forgotten subscriptions and online impulse buys.

en.title: Rising Fixed Costs
en.scenario: Your fixed costs increase by $100/month. How do you react?

choice_liability.label: Accept the increase and randomly cut elsewhere
choice_liability.feedback: Without a specific plan, 'random' cuts always fall on the wrong things — never on the real leaks.

choice_asset.label: Audit all expenses, identify the least useful $100 and eliminate it
choice_asset.feedback: A cost increase is an audit opportunity. You optimize your budget rather than suffering it.

en.title: The Profitable Side Project
en.scenario: Your side project generates its first $300 net this month. What do you do?

choice_liability.label: Finally pay yourself — you deserve it
choice_liability.feedback: Paying yourself from a young project slows its growth. Successful entrepreneurs pay themselves last.

choice_asset.label: Reinvest $250 into the project and keep $50 symbolically
choice_asset.feedback: Early reinvestment accelerates exponential growth. $50 symbolic 'salary' maintains motivation without slowing the project.

en.title: The Subscription Audit
en.scenario: You realize you're paying for 4 streaming services ($55/month total). What do you do?

choice_liability.label: Keep all subscriptions, you use them all 'a bit'
choice_liability.feedback: $55/month = $660/year for passively consumed content. A comparable asset could generate monthly income.

choice_asset.label: Keep 1-2 max, redirect the $30-40 saved to an investment
choice_asset.feedback: Every useless subscription is rent paid for distraction. Optimizing fixed costs frees real investment capital.

en.title: Early Repayment vs Investing
en.scenario: You can pay off $500 of consumer credit this month. Or invest those $500. What do you do?

choice_liability.label: Invest the $500 — returns will cover the credit interest
choice_liability.feedback: Classic math error: consumer credit rates almost always exceed investment returns. Repaying is the priority.

choice_asset.label: Pay off the credit — it's a guaranteed return at the credit rate
choice_asset.feedback: Excellent reasoning. Paying off costly debt offers a guaranteed return equal to its rate. The safest investment right now.

en.title: The Food Budget
en.scenario: You can cut your food bill by $80/month by cooking more. Is it worth it?

choice_liability.label: No, the time it takes isn't worth it
choice_liability.feedback: $80/month = $960/year. Evaluating time/money is fair, but the real barrier is usually habit, not time.

choice_asset.label: Yes, batch cook on weekends and reinvest the savings
choice_asset.feedback: Double benefit: better nutrition + $80/month extra to invest. Batch cooking is an underrated lever.

en.title: The Tech Gadget on Sale
en.scenario: A gadget you've been eyeing is on sale at $400 (down from $600). Is now the time?

choice_liability.label: Yes, -33% is an opportunity not to miss
choice_liability.feedback: A discount on something you hadn't planned to buy isn't a saving — it's a spending disguised as opportunity.

choice_asset.label: No, it wasn't in my plan. I keep the $400 for an asset
choice_asset.feedback: Real financial discipline is resisting unplanned 'good deals'. Your allocation plan overrides promotions.

en.title: The Severance Pay
en.scenario: You leave a job and receive $4,000 in severance. Emergency plan?

choice_liability.label: Travel during the career transition
choice_liability.feedback: Transitions are costly. Using severance to travel without incoming revenue creates unnecessary financial pressure.

choice_asset.label: Build a 3-month emergency fund and invest the rest
choice_asset.feedback: Safety first. A solid emergency fund allows better professional decisions without the pressure of need.

en.title: The Forgotten Balance
en.scenario: You realize you had $600 forgotten in an old bank account. What do you do?

choice_liability.label: The perfect guilt-free treat
choice_liability.feedback: 'Found' money is treated differently than 'earned' money — it's a cognitive bias. In reality, it has the same value.

choice_asset.label: Treat it exactly like any other income and invest it
choice_asset.feedback: Consistent treatment of all money flows is the mark of a real financial plan. Money doesn't change value based on its origin.

en.title: The Savings Challenge
en.scenario: A friend proposes a challenge: save $10 more per week for a year. Do you accept?

choice_liability.label: Too little to change anything, not worth the effort
choice_liability.feedback: $10/week = $520/year. It's not the amount — it's the saver's habit and identity being built.

choice_asset.label: Yes, and I automate a weekly transfer of $10 today
choice_asset.feedback: Automation turns intention into system. Systems beat willpower over time. $520 invested regularly creates real momentum.

en.title: The Security Deposit Refund
en.scenario: You get your $1,200 deposit back when moving. Immediate reflex?

choice_liability.label: Use it to furnish and decorate the new apartment
choice_liability.feedback: Natural — but if the deposit funds the new place, you start from zero financially, same as before.

choice_asset.label: Invest the deposit and use a separate (smaller) budget for the move
choice_asset.feedback: Separating flows prevents 'consuming' refunds. The deposit recovered is capital to preserve, not immediately respend.

en.title: The Modest Inheritance
en.scenario: You inherit $5,000 from a relative. How do you honor that inheritance?

choice_liability.label: Make a travel dream happen in their honor
choice_liability.feedback: The trip can be meaningful and beautiful. But $5,000 entirely spent creates no financial legacy.

choice_asset.label: Invest most of it, make a small symbolic gesture with a portion
choice_asset.feedback: You transform this inheritance into a lasting asset that keeps working. That may be the most respectful way to honor someone's work.

en.title: The Vacation Budget
en.scenario: You save for vacation ($150/month for 8 months). You have $1,200. How do you manage it?

choice_liability.label: Spend all $1,200 for a memorable vacation
choice_liability.feedback: Annual affordable vacations are healthy. But if $1,200 is all your savings, the vacation cost you your entire safety margin.

choice_asset.label: $800 vacation budget, invest $400 of the saved amount
choice_asset.feedback: Enjoy while building. The discipline of not spending everything even when it's 'planned for that' is advanced financial skill.

en.title: The Abandoned Gym
en.scenario: You pay $60/month for gym but barely go. What do you do?

choice_liability.label: Keep the membership, you'll go 'soon'
choice_liability.feedback: $60/month × 12 = $720/year for an intention. Unexecuted intentions are the most expensive expenses.

choice_asset.label: Cancel immediately, reinvest the $60/month, find a free alternative
choice_asset.feedback: Cutting a useless expense and reinvesting it immediately is one of the most concrete financial optimization acts. Act now, not tomorrow.

en.title: Maxed-Out Credit Card
en.scenario: Your credit card is at 90% of its limit ($4,500 balance). Strategy?

choice_liability.label: Pay the minimum each month and keep using the card
choice_liability.feedback: The minimum keeps the balance nearly permanent. Compound interest on consumer debt is the exact inverse of compound investment — just as powerful, but against you.

choice_asset.label: Stop all use, pay the maximum possible each month until paid off
choice_asset.feedback: The debt avalanche: target the most expensive debt first. Every dollar above the minimum is a guaranteed return at the card's rate.

en.title: The Student Loan
en.scenario: You owe $25,000 in student loans at 5%. Method?

choice_liability.label: Pay exactly the minimum installment for the entire term
choice_liability.feedback: At 5%, you're correctly paying the cost of your education. But if you can invest at higher returns, the minimum strategy can be rational — as long as you actually invest the difference.

choice_asset.label: Pay a surplus each month to repay faster and reduce total interest
choice_asset.feedback: Every month of early repayment reduces the term and total interest. It's a guaranteed 5% return on each extra dollar repaid.

en.title: Debt Consolidation
en.scenario: You have 3 debts at different rates. A company offers to merge them into one lower monthly payment. Do you accept?

choice_liability.label: Yes! One lower payment, it's simpler
choice_liability.feedback: Warning: a lower payment over a longer term may mean paying much more in total interest. Calculate the total cost before signing.

choice_asset.label: Analyze the overall rate, total duration and real cost before deciding
choice_asset.feedback: Consolidation can be advantageous — or a marketing trap. The real question: what is the total cost of the consolidated debt vs current repayment?

en.title: Recurring Overdraft
en.scenario: You systematically use your $500 authorized overdraft every month. Reaction?

choice_liability.label: The bank allows it, so it's fine
choice_liability.feedback: Authorized overdrafts are among the most expensive credit in real rate terms. A recurring overdraft signals a worsening cash flow problem.

choice_asset.label: Treat the overdraft as an emergency and restructure my budget immediately
choice_asset.feedback: Recurring overdraft is an alarm signal, not a management tool. It indicates spending exceeds income — a structural problem, not temporary.

en.title: The 0% for 12 Months Offer
en.scenario: A store offers 12-month interest-free payments on a $1,200 purchase. Do you take it?

choice_liability.label: Yes, 0% is free, might as well benefit
choice_liability.feedback: The 0% is real — but only if you repay BEFORE the period ends. After that, the retroactive rate can be extremely high. And you still bought a liability.

choice_asset.label: No, if I can't pay cash, I probably can't afford it
choice_asset.feedback: Kiyosaki's rule: if you have to borrow to buy a liability (consumption), you can't afford it. 0% makes the liability more accessible, not less of a liability.

en.title: The Car Loan
en.scenario: You need a car. The dealer offers a 60-month loan with a 'comfortable' monthly payment. Do you sign?

choice_liability.label: Yes, the payment fits my budget
choice_liability.feedback: A car is a liability that depreciates the moment it leaves the lot. Financing a liability over 60 months means paying interest on something losing value every month.

choice_asset.label: Buy a reliable used car with savings or a short-term loan
choice_asset.feedback: A used car paid in cash or over a short term reduces total cost and monthly pressure. Freeing fixed costs frees capital.

en.title: Avalanche vs Snowball
en.scenario: You have several debts. Which repayment method do you choose?

choice_liability.label: Pay off the smallest ones first to feel progress
choice_liability.feedback: The snowball method (smallest first) is psychologically motivating but mathematically more costly. It pays more total interest.

choice_asset.label: Pay off the highest-rate debt first (avalanche method)
choice_asset.feedback: The avalanche method is mathematically optimal — you pay the least total interest. If you maintain discipline, it's the most efficient strategy.

en.title: The Mortgage Refinance
en.scenario: Rates drop and you can refinance your mortgage to save $150/month. What do you do with the savings?

choice_liability.label: Raise your standard of living with this monthly surplus
choice_liability.feedback: You optimized your debt but absorbed the savings into lifestyle. Optimization only creates wealth if it's reinvested.

choice_asset.label: Reinvest the $150/month saved into a productive asset
choice_asset.feedback: Debt optimization + immediate reinvestment of savings: double wealth lever. You work less and build more in parallel.

en.title: Borrowing to Invest
en.scenario: You can borrow $10,000 at 4% to invest in an asset theoretically returning 8%. Do you do it?

choice_liability.label: Yes, the 4% spread is in my favor
choice_liability.feedback: Leverage amplifies gains AND losses. An 8% return is a hypothesis, not a guarantee. If the asset falls, you still owe the 4% loan.

choice_asset.label: Wait until you have your own funds, or strictly limit leverage
choice_asset.feedback: Investment leverage is an advanced tool to handle with caution. For a beginner, zero leverage is a strategy in itself — safety before optimization.

en.title: Buy Now Pay Later
en.scenario: An online service offers 'buy now, pay in 6 months'. Do you use this option?

choice_liability.label: Yes, it gives me time to gather the funds
choice_liability.feedback: 'Pay in 6 months' often means delaying a purchase you can't afford now. In 6 months, other expenses pile up — and the debt remains.

choice_asset.label: No, if I can't pay now, I don't buy now
choice_asset.feedback: The discipline of immediate payment is a powerful filter against impulse buys. If you can't pay today, the purchase may not be a priority.

en.title: Being a Guarantor
en.scenario: A close friend asks you to co-sign their mortgage. Do you accept?

choice_liability.label: Yes, it's family/a friend, I trust them
choice_liability.feedback: Being a guarantor means legally committing to repay the debt if the borrower defaults. Trust doesn't protect against life's accidents.

choice_asset.label: No, or only after understanding all the legal and financial implications
choice_asset.feedback: Co-signing is a real financial commitment that can affect your future borrowing capacity. A decision deserving analysis, not just love.

en.title: The Debt Buyout
en.scenario: A company offers to buy out all your loans to reduce your payment by $300. Do you accept without hesitation?

choice_liability.label: Yes, $300 less per month is exactly what I need
choice_liability.feedback: A lower payment over a longer term can cost thousands more in total. Calculate the total cost, not just the payment.

choice_asset.label: First calculate the total cost before and after the buyout
choice_asset.feedback: The monthly payment is just one data point. Total cost (amount × duration × rate) is the only metric that matters for comparing loan offers.

en.title: The Family Debt
en.scenario: You owe money to a relative. They're not pressuring you. Do you prioritize repaying them?

choice_liability.label: No, they're not pressing, I'll pay other debts first
choice_liability.feedback: Financially logical if the family debt is at 0%. But not repaying can create an invisible relational tension with a real human cost.

choice_asset.label: Yes, I repay with a clear plan to not let the debt pollute the relationship
choice_asset.feedback: Relational and financial clarity are linked. A formalized repayment plan, even interest-free, respects the trust given and keeps the relationship healthy.

en.title: The Revolving Credit Card
en.scenario: Your bank sends you a revolving credit card with a $2,000 limit. Do you activate it?

choice_liability.label: Yes, it's useful for emergencies and unexpected expenses
choice_liability.feedback: Revolving credit is one of the most expensive financial products. A real emergency fund in a savings account fills the same role with zero interest.

choice_asset.label: No, I build an emergency fund so I never need revolving credit
choice_asset.feedback: The ultimate goal: never need emergency credit. A 3-6 month expense emergency fund is the best financial product that exists.

en.title: Lending to a Friend at Interest
en.scenario: You have $1,000 available. A friend proposes borrowing it at 5% interest. Do you prefer...

choice_liability.label: Lend the $1,000 at 5% to my friend
choice_liability.feedback: 5% guaranteed interest seems attractive. But if the friend doesn't repay, you lose both the money AND the friend. Personal credit risk is often underestimated.

choice_asset.label: Invest the $1,000 in a diversified asset to avoid mixing money and friendship
choice_asset.feedback: Kiyosaki advises never mixing money and emotional relationships without very clear rules. An investment in an asset keeps the friendship pure.

en.title: Mortgage vs Rent
en.scenario: You're torn between buying (mortgage $1,400/month) and renting ($1,100/month). Decision criterion?

choice_liability.label: Buy — it costs more but it's an investment, always better
choice_liability.feedback: Real estate is often presented as an asset, but Kiyosaki nuances: your primary residence generates no income. It can even be a liability if it costs more than it produces.

choice_asset.label: Compare total cost of ownership vs rent + investing the difference
choice_asset.feedback: The real question: if I invest the $300 monthly difference over 20 years, what's the final value vs expected real estate appreciation? The answer isn't obvious.

en.title: The Easy Consumer Credit
en.scenario: A salesperson offers 'easy' credit at $50/month for a $600 purchase. Do you accept?

choice_liability.label: $50/month is nothing, it's affordable
choice_liability.feedback: $50/month may seem negligible, but every consumer credit reduces your savings capacity and potentially your future borrowing capacity for assets.

choice_asset.label: Refuse and wait until you've saved the $600 to pay cash
choice_asset.feedback: The discipline of waiting: if you save $100/month, you have the $600 in 6 months with no interest. Patience is the investor's first capital.

en.title: Rate Renegotiation
en.scenario: You can spend 2 hours renegotiating your mortgage to potentially save $5,000 over the term. Do you do it?

choice_liability.label: 2 hours for hypothetical $5,000 is too much effort
choice_liability.feedback: $5,000 / 2h = $2,500/hour of potential value. Renegotiation is often the financial action with the best ROI available to an individual.

choice_asset.label: Yes, immediately. It's probably the best hour of my financial life
choice_asset.feedback: Mortgage renegotiation is the most accessible and often most ignored financial lever. One hour can be worth thousands — and it's never too late.

en.title: Month-End Card Balance
en.scenario: You can't fully pay your credit card balance this month. How much do you pay?

choice_liability.label: The required minimum, I'll make up for it next month
choice_liability.feedback: Paying the minimum activates compound interest against you. 'Next month' almost never comes for small balances — which silently grow.

choice_asset.label: The maximum possible, and I block the card to not make things worse
choice_asset.feedback: Pay the maximum possible + block usage = double action. Stop the bleeding AND accelerate recovery. The minimum is an illusion of control.

en.title: Training Credit
en.scenario: A course costs $3,000. You can pay cash or use credit at 6%. What do you do?

choice_liability.label: Use credit to keep my cash available for something else
choice_liability.feedback: If 'something else' means spending it, the 6% credit is the wrong decision. If 'something else' means investing at more than 6%, the math may favor credit.

choice_asset.label: Pay cash if the training has a clear and measurable ROI on my income
choice_asset.feedback: Training is an asset if it measurably increases your earning capacity. Cash or credit depends on expected ROI and your alternative interest rate.

en.title: Debt vs Emergency Savings
en.scenario: You have $2,000 saved and $1,500 in debt at 18%. Do you use the savings to repay?

choice_liability.label: No, keep the savings for emergencies, it's safety
choice_liability.feedback: Keeping $2,000 at ~1% while paying 18% on $1,500 costs you ~$250/year net. The 'safety' has a real price.

choice_asset.label: Yes, repay $1,500 and keep $500 as a minimal emergency fund
choice_asset.feedback: Repaying 18% debt with 1% savings is a net gain of 17%. Keep a minimum emergency buffer ($500), repay the rest.

en.title: Credit Insurance
en.scenario: The bank offers credit insurance at $40/month to cover your repayments in case of accident. Do you take it?

choice_liability.label: Yes, it's reassuring and the bank recommends it
choice_liability.feedback: Bank-sold credit insurance is often overpriced and poorly suited. Compare with independent insurance or a self-built emergency fund.

choice_asset.label: Evaluate the real need and compare with independent alternatives
choice_asset.feedback: Insurance can be useful — but the price and conditions vary enormously. The often cheaper alternative: an emergency fund equivalent to 3-6 months of charges.

en.title: Borrowed to Invest
en.scenario: A friend tells you he borrowed to buy speculative assets. He's making a lot. Do you do the same?

choice_liability.label: If he can do it, so can I — the gains look real
choice_liability.feedback: Confirmation bias: you see the gains, not the losses. People don't tell you about leveraged investments that went wrong. Survivorship bias is dangerous.

choice_asset.label: Congratulate the friend, but analyze the real risk of leverage before copying
choice_asset.feedback: Leverage amplifies both directions. Your friend may have been right by luck as much as by strategy. Analyze your own risk profile first.

en.title: Extra Mortgage Payment vs Investing
en.scenario: You can pay $500/month extra on your mortgage (at 3%) OR invest that $500. What do you do?

choice_liability.label: Pay the mortgage off faster to be 'debt-free' sooner
choice_liability.feedback: At 3% mortgage, early repayment offers a guaranteed 3% return. Excellent in low-yield environments, less optimal if you can invest at 7%+.

choice_asset.label: Invest the $500 if expected returns significantly exceed 3%
choice_asset.feedback: Mathematically, if you can invest long-term with an expected return of 6-8% and your mortgage is at 3%, investment wins. But it's risky — the 3% is guaranteed, the 7% isn't.

en.title: Debt Settlement
en.scenario: A company offers to buy your debt for 50 cents on the dollar. Do you accept?

choice_liability.label: Yes! Paying 50% of my debt is the best deal possible
choice_liability.feedback: A 50% settlement may impact your credit history and relationship with creditors. Also check the possible tax implications.

choice_asset.label: Analyze legal, tax and credit score implications before deciding
choice_asset.feedback: Debt settlement can be a viable but complex solution. Understanding all consequences — particularly on future borrowing capacity — is essential.

en.title: The Seller's Free Credit
en.scenario: A real estate seller offers 0% financing for 2 years. Too good to be true?

choice_liability.label: 0% is great, I sign without analyzing the details
choice_liability.feedback: 0% seller credit often compensates with a higher sale price. The financing cost is embedded in the price, not the rate. Negotiate the price first.

choice_asset.label: Analyze whether the price includes the financing cost and negotiate both variables
choice_asset.feedback: A seller offering financing generally embeds that cost in the price. The real question: what would the price be with a cash payment?

en.title: Unable to Pay
en.scenario: You can no longer meet your debt obligations. First action?

choice_liability.label: Wait and hope the situation resolves itself
choice_liability.feedback: Waiting almost always worsens the situation. Interest accumulates, penalties add up, negotiation options shrink.

choice_asset.label: Proactively contact creditors and seek a financial advisor immediately
choice_asset.feedback: Proactive action in a financial crisis is counterintuitive but essential. Creditors generally prefer to negotiate rather than pursue.

en.title: Early Student Debt
en.scenario: You can choose a cheaper university with financial aid or a top school with $60,000 in loans. Decision criterion?

choice_liability.label: Top school — the network and prestige justify the debt
choice_liability.feedback: Prestige has real value in some fields. But $60,000 in student debt represents years of repayment that delay wealth building.

choice_asset.label: Calculate the real ROI of each option: salary differential vs cost of debt
choice_asset.feedback: Education is an investment — like any investment, calculate the return. The expected salary differential should cover the extra debt cost in a reasonable timeframe.

en.title: The Micro Business Loan
en.scenario: You can borrow $5,000 to launch your side business. The project is solid but uncertain. Do you borrow?

choice_liability.label: Yes, no risk no reward, entrepreneurship requires investment
choice_liability.feedback: Calculated risk is healthy. But borrowing for an uncertain project means if it fails, you still owe the repayment. The risk is asymmetric.

choice_asset.label: Bootstrap until you validate the concept, then fund growth with revenues
choice_asset.feedback: Bootstrapping forces business model validation before committing capital. A project that can grow slowly with its own revenues is more robust than a credit-funded one.

en.title: Good Debt vs Bad Debt
en.scenario: Two borrowing opportunities: $10,000 for a luxury car OR $10,000 for a rental property generating income. What's the difference?

choice_liability.label: Both are debts, it's the same either way
choice_liability.feedback: No, Kiyosaki makes a fundamental distinction: good debt finances an asset that generates income greater than the cost of the debt. Bad debt finances a liability.

choice_asset.label: Rental debt is potentially 'good' if rent covers interest and generates surplus
choice_asset.feedback: This is Kiyosaki's key distinction between good and bad debt. Borrowing to acquire a productive asset whose return exceeds the interest rate is a legitimate lever.

en.title: The First $1,000
en.scenario: You've saved $1,000 to invest for the first time. Where do you go?

choice_liability.label: Buy a stock from a company you hear about everywhere
choice_liability.feedback: Following media hype is the most effective way to overpay at the top and panic-sell at the bottom. Popularity often precedes the bubble.

choice_asset.label: Invest in a diversified index fund to start without selection bias
choice_asset.feedback: For a first investment, maximum diversification at low cost is statistically the most solid strategy. It eliminates selection risk and emotional bias.

en.title: The Market Crash
en.scenario: Markets fall 20%. Your portfolio loses $2,000 in 48 hours. What do you do?

choice_liability.label: Sell before it gets worse — better to protect what's left
choice_liability.feedback: Selling during a crash turns a paper loss into a real loss. Historically, investors who sell during crises almost always miss the recovery.

choice_asset.label: Do nothing — or buy more if you have available cash
choice_asset.feedback: Crises are sales for long-term investors. Buffett says to be greedy when others are fearful. Staying the course (and ideally buying more) is counterintuitive but historically winning.

en.title: The Hot Tip
en.scenario: A colleague says they have a 'tip' on a stock that's about to explode. Do you invest?

choice_liability.label: Yes, if they're sure, it's an opportunity not to miss
choice_liability.feedback: Investing on 'tips' exposes you to two risks: the tip is wrong (frequent) or it constitutes insider trading (real legal risk). Trust is not an investment strategy.

choice_asset.label: No, analyze the asset yourself or pass — never on a rumor
choice_asset.feedback: A good investment must withstand personal analysis. If you don't understand why the asset should rise yourself, you won't understand when to sell either.

en.title: Diversification
en.scenario: You've put everything in 2 stocks that have performed well for a year. Strategy?

choice_liability.label: Keep the concentration, it's working and diversifying would dilute gains
choice_liability.feedback: Concentration amplifies gains AND losses. 2 stocks represent extreme portfolio risk. Yesterday's winners aren't guaranteed to be tomorrow's.

choice_asset.label: Progressively diversify toward a portfolio of 15+ uncorrelated assets
choice_asset.feedback: Diversification reduces risk without necessarily reducing expected return. Going from 2 to 20 assets significantly reduces volatility with no major additional cost.

en.title: Real Estate vs Stock Market
en.scenario: You have $30,000 saved. Rental real estate or stock market? Main criterion?

choice_liability.label: Real estate, it's tangible and 'you never lose in real estate'
choice_liability.feedback: Real estate can lose value. And 'tangible' isn't a strategy — it's an emotional bias. Both asset classes have different returns and risks depending on markets.

choice_asset.label: Analyze net return, liquidity and required management level for each option
choice_asset.feedback: Neither is universally superior. The net return of rental real estate (rent - charges - taxes - time) is often lower than it appears.

en.title: Market Timing
en.scenario: An analyst predicts markets will fall in 3 months. Do you wait to invest?

choice_liability.label: Wait for the dip to invest at a better price
choice_liability.feedback: Nobody can reliably predict short-term markets — not analysts, not algorithms. Waiting often means missing gains that offset the anticipated drops.

choice_asset.label: Invest now with your regular plan — time in the market beats timing the market
choice_asset.feedback: 'Time in the market beats timing the market' is one of the most robust conclusions in behavioral finance. Every month out of the market is a lost opportunity.

en.title: Management Fees
en.scenario: You're choosing between a fund with 0.2% annual fees and one with 2% fees but 'actively managed'. Which to choose?

choice_liability.label: The 2% is justified if the manager is expert
choice_liability.feedback: Statistically, the majority of actively managed funds underperform their benchmark index over 10 years, especially after fees. A 1.8% fee difference is enormous over 20 years.

choice_asset.label: The 0.2% fund — studies show low fees are the best predictor of net performance
choice_asset.feedback: 1.8% extra annual fees on $10,000 over 20 years represents a difference of thousands of dollars. Low fees are one of the few advantages an individual investor can control.

en.title: Socially Responsible Investing
en.scenario: You can choose between an ESG fund (less diversified, slightly higher fees) or a broad index fund. What do you prioritize?

choice_liability.label: ESG without hesitation — values before returns
choice_liability.feedback: Values matter. But 'ESG' doesn't guarantee better returns or real impact. Check the ESG methodology and actual fees before choosing.

choice_asset.label: Analyze real impact, fees and historical return before deciding
choice_asset.feedback: Ethical investing is legitimate — but deserves as much rigor as any other investment. Some ESG funds are excellent, others are 'greenwashing' with high fees.

en.title: DCA vs Lump Sum
en.scenario: You have $5,000 to invest. Put it all in at once or spread it over 12 months?

choice_liability.label: Spread over 12 months to 'reduce bad timing risk'
choice_liability.feedback: Mathematically, lump sum (all at once) outperforms DCA (spreading) 2/3 of the time historically, as markets rise more than they fall. But DCA is psychologically better.

choice_asset.label: All at once if markets are 'normal', DCA if very high or very volatile
choice_asset.feedback: Both have their place. Lump sum wins statistically but DCA reduces emotional stress. If emotion might make you sell at the wrong time, DCA may be better in practice.

en.title: Cryptocurrencies
en.scenario: A friend multiplied their crypto investment by 5. Do you want in?

choice_liability.label: Invest heavily — the potential gains are enormous
choice_liability.feedback: Assets that can multiply by 5 can also divide by 5. Crypto is a high-volatility asset class suited for a fraction of a portfolio (5-10% maximum for most).

choice_asset.label: If interested, allocate a limited fraction you're prepared to lose entirely
choice_asset.feedback: The responsible crypto rule: only invest what you're prepared to lose completely. The volatility is real and cycles can be very long.

en.title: Bonds vs Stocks
en.scenario: You're approaching retirement. Do you keep 100% in stocks or rebalance?

choice_liability.label: Keep 100% stocks to maximize gains until the end
choice_liability.feedback: Sequence of returns risk is crucial near retirement: a sharp drop at the wrong moment can dramatically reduce portfolio longevity.

choice_asset.label: Progressively rebalance toward less volatile assets as you approach
choice_asset.feedback: The classic rule: (100 - age)% in stocks. Near retirement, reducing volatility is as important as maximizing returns. Timing risk becomes critical.

en.title: Invest in What You Know
en.scenario: You work in tech. Do you mainly invest in tech companies?

choice_liability.label: Yes, I invest where I have a real informational advantage
choice_liability.feedback: Peter Lynch recommends 'investing in what you know' — but familiarity bias can also lead to over-concentrating in one sector and under-diversifying.

choice_asset.label: Use sector knowledge for selection, but maintain global diversification
choice_asset.feedback: Sector knowledge is a real edge — as long as it serves selection, not over-concentration. Your job already depends on tech: your portfolio shouldn't be 100% correlated with it.

en.title: Annual Rebalancing
en.scenario: After a year of gains, stocks represent 80% of your portfolio instead of 60%. Do you rebalance?

choice_liability.label: No, stocks are performing well, let them grow
choice_liability.feedback: Not rebalancing lets the risk profile drift to more volatility than intended. An 80/20 portfolio is not the same risk as a 60/40.

choice_asset.label: Yes, sell some stocks to return to the target allocation
choice_asset.feedback: Rebalancing systematically enforces 'sell high, buy low'. It's one of the few mechanical actions that produces better risk-adjusted performance.

en.title: Rental Real Estate
en.scenario: You can buy an apartment for $150,000 and rent it for $700/month. Is it a good deal?

choice_liability.label: $700/month is guaranteed income, it's excellent
choice_liability.feedback: $700/month gross ≠ $700 net income. Condo fees, property taxes, vacancy, repairs, management: net return may drop to 2-3%. Calculate NET return.

choice_asset.label: Calculate the net return (rent - all charges - taxes) and compare alternatives
choice_asset.feedback: True rental yield: (annual rent - charges - taxes - vacancy - management) / purchase price. A property at 3% net isn't necessarily better than a low-cost index fund.

en.title: Investing in Your Own Training
en.scenario: An advanced course costs $2,500 and could potentially increase your income by $300/month. Do the math?

choice_liability.label: Too expensive for uncertain results
choice_liability.feedback: $2,500 / $300 = 8.3 months payback. If the training really delivers $300/month extra, it's one of the best returns on investment available.

choice_asset.label: Yes, a ~8-month ROI is excellent — but verify the training's proven results
choice_asset.feedback: Investment in yourself is often the most profitable. But 'potentially' isn't a guarantee. Seek verifiable testimonials and concrete results before investing.

en.title: Concentrated vs Diversified Portfolio
en.scenario: Invest in 5 assets you understand perfectly OR in 50 assets via a fund. What do you prefer?

choice_liability.label: 5 assets I understand well, concentration maximizes gains
choice_liability.feedback: Concentration can maximize gains — and losses. Warren Buffett can do it because he spends 12 hours/day on analysis. For most investors, concentration is excessive risk.

choice_asset.label: 50 diversified assets to reduce the idiosyncratic risk of each
choice_asset.feedback: Diversification is the only free lunch in finance. 50 uncorrelated assets dramatically reduce volatility without necessarily reducing expected long-term return.

en.title: Selling Before the Crash
en.scenario: Experts predict a recession. Do you sell everything to wait it out?

choice_liability.label: Yes, better to protect gains while waiting for certainty
choice_liability.feedback: Certainty doesn't exist in finance. Selling everything creates two problems: when to re-enter? And if the crash doesn't happen, you missed the gain while waiting.

choice_asset.label: Stay invested per my target allocation and maybe increase savings to buy the dip
choice_asset.feedback: Being ready to buy during a dip without panic-selling is the mature investor's strategy. The recession, if it comes, is an opportunity for those with liquidity.

en.title: Start-up Investment
en.scenario: A friend is launching a start-up and looking for investors. He offers you equity for $5,000. Do you join?

choice_liability.label: Yes, start-ups can return 10x if you get in early
choice_liability.feedback: The statistics: 90% of start-ups fail. A $5,000 investment in a friend's start-up has a high probability of going to zero AND creating relationship tension.

choice_asset.label: Evaluate like any investment: business plan, traction, team, and risk of total loss
choice_asset.feedback: A start-up investment deserves as much rigor as a stock market investment. Only invest if you accept the likely total loss and truly understand the business model.

en.title: Liquidity
en.scenario: All your assets are invested long-term and a $1,500 emergency arises. What do you do?

choice_liability.label: Quickly sell part of my investments
choice_liability.feedback: Selling long-term assets for a short-term emergency is exactly what the emergency fund is supposed to prevent. Forced selling at the wrong time crystallizes losses.

choice_asset.label: Use my liquid emergency fund — that's exactly what it's for
choice_asset.feedback: The emergency fund is the buffer that allows you to never touch long-term investments. Its existence protects productive assets from life's surprises.

en.title: ESG in Portfolio
en.scenario: You discover one of your portfolio companies has contested environmental behavior. What do you do?

choice_liability.label: Do nothing — it's their problem, not mine
choice_liability.feedback: As a shareholder, you are a partial owner of the company and its practices. Your investment indirectly finances them.

choice_asset.label: Evaluate whether to sell or hold based on both your values AND financial prospects
choice_asset.feedback: Both criteria can align: contested practices often create regulatory and reputational risks long-term. Ethics and performance aren't always opposed.

en.title: Dividends vs Capital Gains
en.scenario: You prefer dividend stocks (4%/year) or growth stocks (0% dividend but 12% expected growth)?

choice_liability.label: Dividends — it's real regular income
choice_liability.feedback: Dividends are attractive but fiscally less efficient in some countries. A stock growing 12% creates as much value as 4% dividend + 8% price growth.

choice_asset.label: Analyze total return (dividend + appreciation) and tax treatment in your country
choice_asset.feedback: Total return (dividend + growth) is the true metric. Dividend vs growth preference also depends on your tax bracket and current income needs.

en.title: Investing in Gold
en.scenario: During economic uncertainty, an advisor recommends 'putting everything in gold'. Do you agree?

choice_liability.label: Yes, gold is a universal safe haven
choice_liability.feedback: Gold is a store of value, not a productive asset. It generates neither dividend, rent, nor interest. Over the very long term, its real return is close to zero after inflation.

choice_asset.label: Allocate a limited fraction to gold as diversification, not as a main strategy
choice_asset.feedback: Gold has its place in a diversified portfolio as protection against inflation and inverse correlation in crises. But 'everything' in gold replaces one risk with another.

en.title: ETFs vs Active Funds
en.scenario: You must choose between an ETF at 0.15%/year and an active fund claiming to beat the market at 1.5%/year. Criterion?

choice_liability.label: The active fund if its manager has a good reputation
choice_liability.feedback: Past reputation doesn't predict future performance. 90% of active managers underperform their index over 15 years after fees. A 1.35% fee gap is a major handicap.

choice_asset.label: The ETF in the vast majority of cases — data shows the superiority of indices long-term
choice_asset.feedback: Academic studies are unanimous: over 15+ years, more than 90% of active funds underperform their benchmark index after fees. The low-cost ETF wins statistically.

en.title: Asset Allocation at Career Start
en.scenario: You're 25 and starting to invest. What allocation to choose?

choice_liability.label: 50% stocks, 50% bonds for 'balanced risk'
choice_liability.feedback: At 25, you have a 40-year investment horizon. Bonds over-diversify to the downside. At this age, cycle resilience favors a more aggressive allocation in productive assets.

choice_asset.label: Majority allocation in productive assets — the long horizon compensates for volatility
choice_asset.feedback: At 25, time is your best asset. Short-term volatility is absorbed by the decades ahead. An allocation suited to a long horizon maximizes growth potential.

en.title: Financial News
en.scenario: Financial news announces an imminent crisis. Do you check your investments?

choice_liability.label: Yes, monitor daily to react fast if things go wrong
choice_liability.feedback: Studies show the more an investor checks their portfolio, the more they trade (often at the wrong time). Daily monitoring increases anxiety without improving results.

choice_asset.label: No, my strategy is defined. I reassess at most quarterly
choice_asset.feedback: The passionate investor who monitors daily generally underperforms the one who defines their strategy and executes without watching the news. Less noise = better decisions.

en.title: Investing Through a Dedicated Structure
en.scenario: You can invest through a dedicated investment company or holding to optimize your situation. Is it worth the cost of the structure?

choice_liability.label: No, too complex and costly to manage
choice_liability.feedback: For significant amounts, a dedicated structure can represent advantages that greatly exceed management costs. Evaluate based on amounts involved and local regulations.

choice_asset.label: Analyze the break-even between structure costs and real advantages based on amounts and your jurisdiction
choice_asset.feedback: A dedicated investment structure becomes relevant above a certain wealth level. Below that, creation and management costs exceed the benefits — and rules vary greatly by country.

en.title: Investing in Your Own Business
en.scenario: You can invest $10,000 in the stock market or in your own business. What do you choose?

choice_liability.label: Stock market — it's safer and more liquid
choice_liability.feedback: The stock market is liquid but you have no control over companies. Your own business offers total control and potentially the best returns — but also the highest risk.

choice_asset.label: Depends on business stage: early stage goes into the business, mature business → diversify
choice_asset.feedback: The best return opportunities are often in your own early-stage business. As the business matures, progressively diversifying reduces concentration risk.

en.title: The Inherited Portfolio
en.scenario: You inherit a portfolio invested in securities you don't understand. Do you keep or restructure?

choice_liability.label: Keep without changing — it worked for the deceased
choice_liability.feedback: A portfolio suited to someone else (different age, goals, tax situation) isn't necessarily suited to yours. Understanding what you own is a minimum.

choice_asset.label: Understand each position, evaluate if it matches my objectives, restructure if necessary
choice_asset.feedback: Inheriting a portfolio deserves as much analysis as building your own. Aligning inherited investments with your own goals and time horizon is a fiduciary responsibility to yourself.

en.title: The Climate Investment
en.scenario: You believe the energy transition will create the decade's greatest opportunities. How do you invest?

choice_liability.label: All-in on green companies and renewables
choice_liability.feedback: Thematic conviction can be right but timing and selection remain crucial. A sector can have a bright future but already excessive valuations — which reduces future returns.

choice_asset.label: Allocate a portfolio fraction to this theme while maintaining a diversified base
choice_asset.feedback: A thematic conviction justifies overweighting, not total concentration. Keeping a diversified base protects against the possibility that the theme is right but the timing is wrong.

en.title: Direct Real Estate vs REITs
en.scenario: You want real estate exposure. Direct property or listed real estate fund?

choice_liability.label: Direct real estate — it's tangible and you control everything
choice_liability.feedback: Direct real estate offers total control but also active management, low liquidity and geographic concentration. It's not a passive asset.

choice_asset.label: Evaluate both by liquidity, diversification, net return and management time
choice_asset.feedback: Listed real estate funds offer diversification, liquidity and zero management. Direct property offers leverage, control and potential tax advantages. Both have their place by profile.

en.title: The E vs B Reflex
en.scenario: You have a business idea but your current job is stable and well paid. What do you do?

choice_liability.label: Keep the stable job — safety first
choice_liability.feedback: The Employee (E) mentality values the paycheck's security. Stability has real value — but it can also become a ceiling preventing real financial freedom.

choice_asset.label: Test the idea in parallel evenings/weekends before making the leap
choice_asset.feedback: The B (Business Owner) quadrant often starts outside work hours. Testing at low risk before jumping is the strategy of most successful entrepreneurs.

en.title: The Definition of Risk
en.scenario: Your circle says 'investing is risky'. How do you react?

choice_liability.label: They're right — better to keep money in a safe savings account
choice_liability.feedback: Not investing also has a risk: the inflation risk that erodes purchasing power, and the risk of not reaching long-term financial goals.

choice_asset.label: Not investing is also a risk — losing purchasing power and decades of growth
choice_asset.feedback: Kiyosaki says financial education reduces risk. What's risky for someone without training isn't risky for someone who understands what they're doing.

en.title: Money and Emotions
en.scenario: You make a bad financial decision and lose $500. How do you handle it?

choice_liability.label: Punish yourself mentally and avoid taking risks for months
choice_liability.feedback: Loss aversion is the most documented behavioral bias. An investor who self-punishes after a mistake makes worse future decisions through over-caution.

choice_asset.label: Objectively analyze the mistake, draw a lesson from it and move on
choice_asset.feedback: The best investors make mistakes — and draw lessons without drowning in them emotionally. Post-mistake objectivity is a skill that's cultivated.

en.title: Visible Wealth
en.scenario: Your neighbor buys a luxury car. What's your inner reaction?

choice_liability.label: They've made it — they can afford it, I should work harder
choice_liability.feedback: The wealthy neighbor syndrome. Studies on millionaires show real wealth is often invisible: modest cars, moderate homes, wealth in non-displayed assets.

choice_asset.label: The car proves nothing — real wealth is in invisible assets
choice_asset.feedback: 'The Millionaire Next Door' documents that most truly rich people don't display it. A luxury car can signal real wealth or well-hidden debt.

en.title: Financial Education
en.scenario: You have 2 free hours this weekend. What do you do?

choice_liability.label: Watch a series or rest — you worked hard this week
choice_liability.feedback: Rest is legitimate and necessary. But 2 hours of financial educational content per week can transform your relationship with money over 1 year.

choice_asset.label: Read a book or listen to a podcast on finance and investing
choice_asset.feedback: Kiyosaki says the difference between the rich and others is what they do with their free time. Financial education is an investment in yourself that pays for life.

en.title: Advice from the Wealthy
en.scenario: You have the opportunity to have lunch with a very wealthy person. What question do you ask?

choice_liability.label: How did you get started? What was your big break?
choice_liability.feedback: The 'lucky break' question looks for the shortcut. But most lasting fortunes result from methodical construction, not a single dice roll.

choice_asset.label: What mistake would you spare me? What habit most changed your trajectory?
choice_asset.feedback: Questions about mistakes and habits give actionable information. Those who succeed long-term generally have systems and habits more than lucky breaks.

en.title: The Fear of Starting
en.scenario: You want to invest but think 'not yet, I need to know more first'. Since 2 years. What do you do?

choice_liability.label: Keep learning — better to be sure before starting
choice_liability.feedback: Perfect waiting is the enemy of imperfect reality. 2 years of non-investing has a real opportunity cost. Perfect knowledge doesn't exist — action with uncertainty does.

choice_asset.label: Start small with what you know, and learn by doing
choice_asset.feedback: Real financial skills develop in practice, not just theory. A small real investment teaches more than a hundred hours of reading.

en.title: Criticism from Those Around You
en.scenario: You decide to invest and your family says 'you'll lose everything'. How do you react?

choice_liability.label: Give up or keep your plans secret to avoid conflict
choice_liability.feedback: Hiding your financial projects from those around you or giving in to social pressure both allow your environment to control your financial trajectory.

choice_asset.label: Listen to the concern, analyze it rationally, then decide based on your own research
choice_asset.feedback: Family criticism often comes from fear, not expertise. Listen without automatically obeying. Analyze the substance of the argument, not the emotional form.

en.title: Time vs Money
en.scenario: You can accept an extra assignment for $500 more this month. But it would take 20 hours of your free time. Do you accept?

choice_liability.label: Yes, $500 is $500 — you don't refuse money
choice_liability.feedback: $500 / 20h = $25/h. The real question: how much is your free time worth? And could those 20 hours be used to build something that pays more long-term?

choice_asset.label: Depends on my finances, and what I'd do with that time otherwise
choice_asset.feedback: Time is the non-renewable asset. At $25/h, the assignment may be worth it — or not. The decision should factor in what 20 hours of free time could create alternatively.

en.title: The Shame of Talking About Money
en.scenario: You never talk about money with friends for fear of seeming 'obsessed'. Is that healthy?

choice_liability.label: Yes, money is a private subject you shouldn't bring up
choice_liability.feedback: The money taboo is culturally constructed — and very costly. It prevents sharing good practices, discovering common mistakes and helping each other financially.

choice_asset.label: No, talking about money with kindness is a source of collective learning
choice_asset.feedback: People who openly discuss money with their circle learn faster and make fewer mistakes. The financial taboo protects those who sell expensive and poorly-suited products.

en.title: Financial Identity
en.scenario: You describe yourself as 'someone who isn't good with money'. Is that true?

choice_liability.label: Yes, some people just have that gift and others don't
choice_liability.feedback: Negative financial identity is a self-fulfilling prophecy. Believing you 'aren't good with money' prevents learning and changing your behaviors.

choice_asset.label: No, it's a learnable skill — not an innate character trait
choice_asset.feedback: Financial literacy is a skill, not an innate talent. Kiyosaki was poor before he was rich. Good financial habits can be learned at any age.

en.title: Jealousy of Wealth
en.scenario: You see a peer's financial success and feel jealousy. How do you transform it?

choice_liability.label: Avoid seeing them — their success makes you uncomfortable
choice_liability.feedback: Avoided jealousy stagnates. It doesn't transform into motivation without conscious reflection on what it reveals about your own desires and blocks.

choice_asset.label: Use jealousy as a signal to identify what you truly want and how to get it
choice_asset.feedback: Jealousy is an emotional compass. It points toward something you value but don't have. Used consciously, it becomes motivation and clarification of priorities.

en.title: Generosity and Wealth
en.scenario: You think being rich means being greedy. Would Kiyosaki agree?

choice_liability.label: Yes, the rich keep everything for themselves, that's why they get rich
choice_liability.feedback: The wealth-greed correlation is a myth. Studies on millionaires show generosity is more prevalent among the wealthy than among those seeking to appear wealthy.

choice_asset.label: No, generosity and wealth are compatible — real wealth comes from surplus, not deprivation
choice_asset.feedback: Kiyosaki emphasizes that real wealth creates abundance to share. The generosity of a wealthy person with productive assets costs less proportionally than generosity forced by guilt.

en.title: Daily Financial Discipline
en.scenario: You have a monthly budget that you often don't stick to. Why?

choice_liability.label: Because life is unpredictable and budgets are too rigid
choice_liability.feedback: A budget can be flexible without being ignored. The unpredictable can be budgeted (emergency fund, miscellaneous budget). The real problem is often lack of tracking, not rigidity.

choice_asset.label: Because the budget isn't automated and willpower alone isn't enough
choice_asset.feedback: Willpower is a muscle that exhausts. Automatic systems (automatic transfers, categorized budgets through apps) replace willpower with reliable mechanisms.

en.title: Money and Happiness
en.scenario: A friend says: 'Money doesn't buy happiness'. What do you say?

choice_liability.label: They're right, happiness comes from relationships and experiences, not money
choice_liability.feedback: Partially true. But below a certain threshold of financial security, lack of money causes real chronic stress that directly affects happiness.

choice_asset.label: Money doesn't buy happiness, but its absence can create a lot of suffering
choice_asset.feedback: Psychology research shows money increases happiness up to a security threshold. Below that threshold, financial problems are the number one source of chronic stress.

en.title: Financial Freedom as a Goal
en.scenario: Your 10-year goal: financial freedom or career promotion?

choice_liability.label: Promotion — it's concrete, measurable and socially valued
choice_liability.feedback: Promotion increases salary — an active income. Financial freedom aims for passive income. Both can coexist, but promotion alone doesn't automatically lead to freedom.

choice_asset.label: The two aren't opposed — use the promotion to accelerate financial freedom
choice_asset.feedback: Promotion can fund the investment that leads to freedom. The mistake is spending the raise rather than investing it. Promotion is an accelerator, not a destination.

en.title: Patience and Wealth
en.scenario: You want quick financial results. Is it realistic?

choice_liability.label: Yes, with the right investments you can get rich fast
choice_liability.feedback: The quest for quick wealth is the source of most financial scams. Exceptional short-term returns imply exceptional risk — often poorly understood.

choice_asset.label: Real wealth is slow and predictable — shortcuts almost always cost more
choice_asset.feedback: Lasting wealth is built over decades, not months. The world's wealthiest people have massively benefited from decades of compound interest, not strokes of genius.

en.title: The Financial Mistake
en.scenario: You realize you overpaid for an asset. Do you sell at a loss or hold?

choice_liability.label: Hold to not 'realize the loss'
choice_liability.feedback: This is the loss aversion bias. A paper loss is a real economic loss regardless of the accounting column. Holding a bad asset hoping for a return is irrational if the analysis changes.

choice_asset.label: Objectively analyze if the prospects justify holding — independent of the price paid
choice_asset.feedback: The analysis of 'what would I do if I didn't already have this asset? Would I buy it today?' eliminates the paid price bias. The decision must be based on future prospects, not past cost.

en.title: Hard Work vs System
en.scenario: You believe working harder than everyone is the key to financial success. Would Kiyosaki agree?

choice_liability.label: Yes, effort and perseverance are the absolute key
choice_liability.feedback: Hard work is necessary but insufficient. Kiyosaki notes that the hardest-working employees are often the least wealthy, because their effort enriches the company, not their own asset column.

choice_asset.label: No, Kiyosaki insists: the right system > hard work alone
choice_asset.feedback: 'Work hard for yourself, not for someone else' is the central message of Rich Dad Poor Dad. Building systems (business, assets) that work without you is true freedom.

en.title: Social Comparison
en.scenario: Your friends all seem to be 'succeeding' on social media. Does it affect you financially?

choice_liability.label: Yes, their posts push me to spend to appear at the same level
choice_liability.feedback: Social media-fueled comparison is a powerful driver of impulsive spending. You pay real expenses for a virtual image.

choice_asset.label: No, I know social media shows everyone's best — I only compare myself to my past self
choice_asset.feedback: Comparing your reality to others' highlight reel is an illusion. The only relevant comparison: are you in better shape than a year ago? Personal progress is the true metric.

en.title: Delegation
en.scenario: You manage everything yourself to 'avoid scams'. Is it optimal?

choice_liability.label: Yes, nobody manages my money better than me
choice_liability.feedback: Over-centralization is a bottleneck. Very wealthy people delegate execution while maintaining strategic oversight.

choice_asset.label: Delegate the execution of certain financial tasks while keeping strategic oversight
choice_asset.feedback: Scalable wealth involves delegation. Accountant, advisor, property manager: delegating execution frees your time for high-value decisions.

en.title: Learning from Others' Mistakes
en.scenario: You know someone who went bankrupt. How do you use that experience?

choice_liability.label: Avoid everything they did, even if contexts are different
choice_liability.feedback: Copying someone else's fear without analyzing the context is as irrational as copying their actions without reflection. Each financial situation is unique.

choice_asset.label: Analyze the structural causes of the failure to extract applicable principles
choice_asset.feedback: Others' failures are free lessons. Understanding the mechanisms (excessive leverage, lack of liquidity, concentration) more than the specific anecdotes extracts transferable principles.

en.title: Luxury and Value
en.scenario: You're hesitating about a $800 luxury item. How do you decide?

choice_liability.label: Luxury rewards hard work — you deserve it if you set the goal
choice_liability.feedback: Material reward isn't problematic in itself — if budgeted and proportional to built assets. The problem is when luxury precedes assets.

choice_asset.label: Buy if my assets cover it easily — not if it compromises my monthly investment
choice_asset.feedback: Kiyosaki's rule: the rich buy luxury last, with income from their assets. The poor and middle class buy luxury first, with their active income.

en.title: The Employee's Freedom
en.scenario: Your boss offers a great promotion with more responsibility but more constraints. Do you accept?

choice_liability.label: Yes, promotion is the natural career progression
choice_liability.feedback: A promotion that further chains you in the time-for-money exchange isn't progress toward freedom. It's progress within the E/S quadrant.

choice_asset.label: Accept if the promotion funds an asset project — decline if it consumes more time without creating independence
choice_asset.feedback: Promotion is neither good nor bad — it's what you do with it. If it funds an investment that reduces salary dependence, it's leverage. Otherwise, it's a golden chain.

en.title: Abundance Mentality
en.scenario: A competitor copies your business idea. First reaction?

choice_liability.label: It's a betrayal — you'll do everything to block them
choice_liability.feedback: The scarcity mentality sees competition as an existential threat. The abundance mentality sees it as market validation and an opportunity to differentiate.

choice_asset.label: Competition validates the market — focus on what differentiates you
choice_asset.feedback: A competitor copying your idea proves the market exists. Kiyosaki talks about abundance: there's enough success for everyone. The race to differentiation is more productive than legal battle.

en.title: First Job and Money
en.scenario: You get your first job at 22. First financial decision?

choice_liability.label: Set up comfortably: nice apartment, car, outings
choice_liability.feedback: The first paycheck trap: lifestyle inflation from the start. Habits established at 22 will be hard to change at 30. Starting small and investing the difference is a well-documented strategy.

choice_asset.label: Automate 20% of salary to investment from the first month, before getting used to the amount
choice_asset.feedback: Pay yourself first is Kiyosaki's principle. Before getting used to the full salary, automatically allocating a portion to investment creates a foundational habit.

en.title: Respect for Money
en.scenario: You receive $20 too much in change at the supermarket. What do you do?

choice_liability.label: Keep it — it's not your fault the cashier made a mistake
choice_liability.feedback: A values question beyond finance. But how you treat money in small amounts reflects your relationship with money in general.

choice_asset.label: Point it out and return the change — integrity applies to small amounts too
choice_asset.feedback: Kiyosaki talks about respecting money in all its forms. Financial integrity in small things is the foundation of self-trust in big decisions.

en.title: The E→S→B→I Mindset
en.scenario: You're an employee and want to become an 'asset owner'. Where do you start?

choice_liability.label: Immediately quit your job to launch something
choice_liability.feedback: The immediate jump from E to B without a safety net is risky. Most successful entrepreneurs prepared their transition alongside their job, not by quitting abruptly.

choice_asset.label: Build progressively: E → side project S → business B → passive assets I in parallel
choice_asset.feedback: Kiyosaki's quadrant isn't a jump — it's a progression. Each step builds on the previous. The transition is prepared while still in security.

en.title: Refusing Mediocrity
en.scenario: You wonder if aiming for financial freedom is arrogant or unrealistic. Does that stop you?

choice_liability.label: A little yes — who am I to want more than average?
choice_liability.feedback: The normalization of financial mediocrity is culturally constructed. Asking 'who am I to want more?' is a mental limit with a real financial cost over decades.

choice_asset.label: No — aiming for financial freedom isn't arrogant, it's responsible
choice_asset.feedback: Financial freedom isn't a luxury — it's the ability to choose your time, your projects and your contributions. Aspiring to it is an act of responsibility to yourself and your loved ones.

en.title: The Definition of Success
en.scenario: How do you define financial success? High salary or time freedom?

choice_liability.label: High salary — recognition and compensation prove value
choice_liability.feedback: A high salary is success in the E quadrant. But Kiyosaki questions: if you stopped working tomorrow, would your lifestyle change? If yes, you're not yet financially free.

choice_asset.label: Time freedom — having assets that cover my expenses without me working
choice_asset.feedback: This is Kiyosaki's definition: financial freedom is when your assets generate more than your expenses. Salary is a means, not an end. Time freedom is the destination.

en.title: The Unexpected Food Budget
en.scenario: You realize you spend $400/month on food and delivery, $200 more than planned. What do you do?

choice_liability.label: That's normal, life is expensive — I adjust my budget upward
choice_liability.feedback: Adjusting the budget upward to justify an unoptimized expense is the reverse of financial management. The budget guides expenses, not the other way around.

choice_asset.label: Identify the $200 in leaks (impulse deliveries, waste) and redirect them
choice_asset.feedback: $200/month = $2,400/year = a significant investment. Analyzing leaks and redirecting to an asset is a few-minutes decision for a major annual impact.

es.title: El Bono Inesperado
es.scenario: Recibes un bono de $3,000 en el trabajo. ¿Qué haces?

choice_liability.label: Comprar el último smartphone + auriculares
choice_liability.feedback: Acabas de convertir $3,000 en algo que vale $1,800 mañana. Kiyosaki llama a esto alimentar la columna de pasivos.

choice_asset.label: Invertir en un fondo índice que genera ingresos mensuales
